title: Make-CLI Conformance Hardening
description: Recent inferctl improvements need to move from demo-proven behavior to product-owned machine contracts. Agents need one discoverable preflight command before local model work, and dashboards or monitors need a stable aggregate status feed whose schema, exit codes, docs, and goldens match implementation behavior.
design: Treat the preflight report and status feed as first-class product contracts rather than shell compositions. Keep every machine surface on the universal JSON envelope with `ok`, `tool_version`, `data`, `meta`, `warnings`, `commands`, and `errors`. Keep the work control-plane-only: configuration, reachability, route selection, model inventory, warning codes, prompt metadata, and policy decisions are in scope; inference, warmups, benchmarks, hidden data-plane probes, prompt content emission, and model loading are out of scope.
acceptance_criteria:
- `preflight` is a first-class inferctl surface with JSON, Markdown, schemas, capabilities, docs, and goldens.
- `status --json` and `status --json --watch` are the product-supported aggregate live-state feed for agents, dashboards, and external monitors.
- `dashboard` remains documented and tested as a human renderer over the public status feed, not as a machine contract.
- Capabilities, generated docs, schema export, contract goldens, and exit-code dictionaries agree with actual command behavior.
- Demo scripts are thin examples over product contracts and do not invent CLI policy in shell.
- Failure paths emit structured JSON when requested, mirror diagnostics to stderr, and exit with documented codes.
notes: Epic rollup: completed preflight as a first-class JSON/Markdown/schema/capabilities/docs/golden surface; pinned preflight runnability and failure semantics; thinned the preflight demo to product commands; normalized status_frame and status_event_batch contracts; unified status event classification with diff; pinned status watch/event/cancellation behavior; preserved dashboard as a human TUI over status --json --watch; updated help/robot docs/agent docs; and passed the final conformance gate.
close_reason: All child beads completed and verified. The epic delivered first-class preflight and status machine contracts, dashboard human-boundary protections, aligned schemas/capabilities/docs/goldens, thin demo behavior, structured failure semantics, and a passing conformance gate. Final verification included go test ./..., scripts/check-contract-goldens.sh, git diff --check, built-binary help/capabilities/schema checks, jq validation, and demo-4 execution.

title: Implement Preflight Machine Contract
description: Agents need a single first-try command before local model jobs that reports whether automation may proceed, why, and what command to run next. The durable behavior currently implied by demo orchestration needs to live in inferctl itself.
design: Use or harden `inferctl preflight <task> --prompt-file <path> --json` and `inferctl preflight <task> --prompt-file <path> --format markdown`. The JSON response should use the universal envelope. The `data` payload should include `preflight_schema_version`, `task`, `runnable`, `runnability_status`, `policy`, `prompt`, `route`, `summary`, and `recommended_action`. Prompt data must be metadata only: source, file basename where relevant, character count, estimated token count, optional hash, and context-budget pressure. Preflight may read prompt text only to compute metadata and optional content hash; it must not persist prompt content, emit prompt content, send prompt content to a backend, or judge output quality from prompt content.
acceptance_criteria:
- `inferctl preflight <task> --prompt-file <path> --json` emits the universal envelope with populated `meta`, `warnings`, `commands`, and `errors` arrays.
- `inferctl preflight <task> --prompt-file <path> --format markdown` renders from the same data structure as JSON.
- `--allow-fallback` and `--require-ready` affect only the runnability decision; they do not change route selection and do not trigger inference or model loading.
- `commands` includes paste-ready safe follow-ups such as backend, route, model, or future warmup/load recommendations without performing those actions.
- Invalid prompt-source combinations fail with structured errors and a copy-pasteable correction.
notes:
CLOSED:
- Preflight JSON data now exposes schema/version, top-level runnable status, route, and summary fields while preserving legacy route_decision/runnability fields.
- Preflight emits paste-ready route/backend/model follow-up commands in the standard envelope commands array.
- Prompt-source validation now emits a structured correction.
- Verified with go test ./... before close.
close_reason: Hardened preflight machine JSON contract with schema/version, runnable aliases, route/summary fields, command recommendations, and prompt-source diagnostics — commit 8285e26

If any verification command depends on the local environment instead of deterministic fixture servers, add focused tests or fixture support before closing the epic.

- `go test ./...` passes.
- `scripts/check-contract-goldens.sh` passes.
- `git diff --check` passes.
- `inferctl preflight --help`, `inferctl status --help`, and `inferctl dashboard --help` run successfully.
- `inferctl capabilities --json | jq empty` passes.
- `inferctl schema --json | jq empty` passes.
- `examples/demo-4-agent-preflight-report.sh` passes.

No implementation changes were needed for this gate.

title: Pin Preflight Runnability Semantics
description: Preflight needs a deterministic runnability decision and documented process exits so automation can decide whether to proceed without scraping human text.
design: Publish preflight exit codes in `capabilities --json`: `0 success` when automation may attempt the task, `1 user_input_error` for invalid invocation or policy blocks, `3 tool_environment_error` for missing or unreadable local configuration/state, and `4 transient_failure` for retryable backend or probe failures. Do not use exit `2` for preflight readiness failures unless the global exit-code dictionary is intentionally broadened in the same change.
acceptance_criteria:
- Runnable primary route returns `ok: true`, exits `0`, and reports `data.runnability.status` as `runnable`.
- Fallback route with `--allow-fallback` returns `ok: true`, exits `0`, and reports `runnable`.
- Fallback route without `--allow-fallback` returns `ok: false`, exits `1`, reports `policy_blocked`, and emits `E_PREFLIGHT_POLICY_BLOCKED`.
- Selected model not loaded with `--require-ready` returns `ok: false`, exits `1`, reports `policy_blocked`, and emits `E_PREFLIGHT_POLICY_BLOCKED`.
- Unknown task and multiple prompt sources return `ok: false`, exit `1`, and emit structured invocation errors.
- Missing, unreadable, invalid, or validation-failing config returns `ok: false`, exits `3`, and emits the corresponding config error code.
- No viable route because candidates are unavailable returns `ok: false`, exits `4`, reports `transient_error`, and emits `E_NO_ROUTE_AVAILABLE`.
- Golden fixtures cover runnable primary, fallback used, fallback blocked, selected model not ready with `--require-ready`, unknown task, invalid config, and no route available.
notes:
CLOSED:
- Preflight runnability and exit semantics are pinned by focused tests for success, policy blocks, invocation errors, config/environment errors, and transient no-route failures.
- Verified with go test ./... before close.
close_reason: Pinned preflight runnability statuses, exit classes, and structured error codes across success, policy, invocation, config, and transient failures — commit 6173c02

title: Normalize Status Frame Contract
description: The status feed needs stable vocabulary, hash semantics, and privacy rules so `status --json` can be the product-supported aggregate live-state feed without conflicting with persisted snapshot/diff artifacts.
design: Prefer `status_frame` for live status records and reserve `control_plane_snapshot` for persisted comparable artifacts from `inferctl snapshot` and `inferctl diff`. Move status-frame capture time out of `data` and use envelope `meta.ts_iso` for call time so `meta.data_hash` changes only when stable control-plane state changes. Define status-frame data as an allowlist and redact or hash fields that are needed for the dashboard but should not expose secrets, prompt text, full prompt file paths, arbitrary local filesystem paths, raw config files, auth material, or non-public endpoints.
acceptance_criteria:
- The machine contract uses `status_frame`, or retains `status_snapshot` only with a documented compatibility reason and deprecation path.
- Status frame capture time is not part of `data`; call time lives in envelope metadata.
- Repeated status calls against unchanged fixture state produce stable `meta.data_hash` values.
- Status-frame schema notes define allowed fields and prohibited fields.
- Tests assert that status frames exclude prompt text, auth headers, tokens, API keys, secret config values, full prompt file paths, arbitrary local filesystem paths, and raw config files.
notes:
CLOSED:
- Status feed now uses status_frame_schema_version/status_frame schema vocabulary.
- Status frame data omits capture time; call time remains in envelope metadata and data_hash is stable for unchanged state.
- Status frame privacy allowlist is documented and tested for prompt/config/secret/path exclusions.
- Dashboard feed parsing supports the new frame discriminator.
- Verified with go test ./... and scripts/check-contract-goldens.sh.
close_reason: Normalized status frame contract: status_frame schema vocabulary, no capture time in data, stable data_hash, privacy allowlist docs/tests, dashboard parser update — commit 8b12ee3

title: Pin Status Watch And Events
description: Watch-mode consumers need a documented newline-delimited envelope stream, complete capabilities metadata, and goldens for both status frames and event batches.
design: Update the `status` capabilities entry to include every implemented exit code, including `1 user_input_error` for invalid `--interval` and invalid `--events` usage. Document and test `status --json --watch` as newline-delimited standard envelopes. Status frame records should carry `data.status_frame_schema_version`; event batch records should carry `data.event_schema_version`. `--events` should be valid only with `--watch`, and `--interval` should be a positive duration.
acceptance_criteria:
- `status` capabilities exit codes match implementation behavior, including invalid `--interval` and invalid `--events` cases.
- `status_frame` and `status_event_batch` schemas are exported and present in capabilities.
- Status-frame and event-batch goldens are pinned.
- Watch tests cover newline-delimited envelopes, event batches, cancellation, invalid interval, and `--events` without `--watch`.
- SIGINT, SIGTERM, and caller/test-harness cancellation after at least one complete frame exit `0` without emitting a partial final JSON record.
- Robot docs include a paste-ready recipe for consuming the status stream.
notes: Pinned status watch/event contract. Added status_event_batch golden, capabilities assertions for status exit codes and exported status_frame/status_event_batch schemas, status watch tests for newline-delimited frames, event batches, caller cancellation, and injected SIGINT/SIGTERM cancellation without partial JSON records. Updated status capabilities/docs to include exit code 1 and adjusted status_event before/after schema to classifier scalar values. Verified go test ./cmd/inferctl -run 'TestStatusWatch|TestStatusEvent|TestStatusEvents', go test ./internal/contract focused tests, go test ./..., scripts/check-contract-goldens.sh, and git diff --check.
close_reason: Implemented status watch/event contract hardening in commit c2f3a90. Status capabilities/docs now include exit code 1, status_frame and status_event_batch schemas remain exported and asserted, status_event_batch has a committed golden, watch tests cover newline-delimited frames, event batches, invalid interval, --events without --watch, caller cancellation, and injected SIGINT/SIGTERM cancellation without partial final JSON. title: Share Status Change Classification
description: Status event generation and `diff` change classification should not drift. They need one owner for change severity, ranking, and explanations across watch feeds, TUI rendering, and `inferctl diff`.
design: Unify status event generation and `diff` change classification behind one internal control-plane change-classification module, or document a deliberate reason they must diverge. The preferred shape is a normalized current-state type that can be fed by both `status_frame` and `control_plane_snapshot`, plus a classifier that produces backend reachability, selected-route, fallback-status, readiness, warning-code, error-code, and recommended-action changes.
acceptance_criteria:
- Status events and diff reports render from the same classified change records, or a documented compatibility decision explains why they intentionally diverge.
- The shared classifier covers backend reachability, selected route, fallback status, readiness, warning code, error code, and recommended-action changes.
- Tests show event severity, ranking, and explanations remain stable across watch output and diff output for equivalent state changes.
notes: Implemented via shared control-plane classifier: status events now adapt controlPlaneChange records from classifyControlPlaneChanges for global backend/model/warning/action changes and per-task route/fallback/readiness changes. Removed the older bespoke status-event diff helpers. Updated status_event kind schema and contract README. Verified go test ./cmd/inferctl -run 'TestStatusEvents|TestDiff|TestStatusEventBatchEnvelope', go test ./..., scripts/check-contract-goldens.sh, and git diff --check.
close_reason: Implemented shared status/diff control-plane change classification in commit 0419403. Status events now derive kind, severity, ranking, explanation, subject, and before/after values from controlPlaneChange records, with schema/docs/tests covering backend reachability, selected route, fallback, readiness, warning-code, error-code, recommended-action, and loaded-model count changes. Verified focused status/diff tests, go test ./..., contract goldens, and git diff --check.

title: Build Agent Stub Demo
description:
Agent builders need a compact, runnable example that shows how an agent can use inferctl for local model routing without sending inference traffic through inferctl. The demo should make the control-plane/data-plane boundary visible: inferctl chooses and explains the backend/model route, then the example agent calls the selected backend directly.

The demo requires these inferctl primitives:

```sh
inferctl preflight code --prompt-file task.txt --allow-fallback --json
inferctl config show --json
```

`preflight` is the readiness gate for automation and includes the selected route decision. `config show` maps the selected backend name to connection metadata such as `base_url`. `route --json` is still useful as an optional diagnostic command when a reader wants the full route explanation by itself, but the demo script should not need both `preflight` and `route` to choose a backend. `capabilities --json` may be used for compatibility checks, but it must not be treated as live backend inventory or route selection.

This plan assumes an inferctl build that includes `preflight`. If a demo script runs against an older binary without `preflight`, it should fail before any backend call with a clear version/compatibility message rather than silently falling back to route-only behavior.
design:
Ship a tiny Python variant and a tiny TypeScript variant under `examples/agent-discovery/`. Each script should:

- Accept a user prompt or task text from the CLI.
- Run `inferctl preflight code --prompt-file <file> --allow-fallback --json` before attempting inference.
- Print the selected backend, selected model, fallback status, and route reason.
- Load `inferctl config show --json`, resolve the selected backend's `base_url`, and call that backend directly with an OpenAI-compatible client.
- Keep inferctl out of the data plane: no chat/completion traffic should be proxied through inferctl.

Canonical JSON paths for this demo:

- `preflight --json`: `data.route_decision.selected_backend`, `data.route_decision.selected_model`, `data.route_decision.is_fallback`, `data.route_decision.ready`, and `data.route_decision.reason`.
- `config show --json`: `data.effective_config.backends[<selected_backend>].base_url`.
- Optional `route --json` diagnostics: `data.decision.*` mirrors the same route-decision fields without the preflight runnability wrapper.

Use the OpenAI SDKs directly because most supported local backends expose OpenAI-compatible HTTP surfaces. Avoid LangChain, LlamaIndex, tool-use, REPL behavior, or framework scaffolding in the first version; the demo should teach one concept in one scroll.
acceptance_criteria:
- `examples/agent-discovery/demo.py` runs a one-shot prompt, prints the inferctl handoff before any backend call, and prints a model response when a compatible backend completion endpoint is available.
- `examples/agent-discovery/demo.ts` provides the same handoff and direct-backend call pattern, including response printing when a compatible backend completion endpoint is available.
- Both scripts fail before inference when `preflight` returns non-runnable, preserving the preflight exit reason in user-facing output.
- Both scripts can proceed when `preflight` selects a fallback only if the script has explicitly passed `--allow-fallback`.
- Neither script uses `inferctl capabilities --json` as the source of backend/model selection.
- Documentation states that inferctl makes control-plane decisions and the selected backend receives the data-plane request directly.
notes: Closed: agent stub demo completed via children inferctl-4lg.1 through inferctl-4lg.4. Implementation commit dfee328 adds Python and TypeScript examples, deterministic handoff harness, and README documentation.
close_reason: Agent stub demo complete: Python/TypeScript handoff examples, fixture harness, and docs delivered — commit dfee328

title: Implement Python Stub
description: Python is one of the primary agent-builder languages, so the hero demo needs a minimal Python implementation that is readable in a README and runnable from a source checkout.
design:
Create `examples/agent-discovery/demo.py`. Keep the script dependency surface limited to Python standard library plus the OpenAI Python client. The script should create a temporary prompt file or accept `--prompt-file`, call inferctl JSON commands via `subprocess`, parse the JSON envelope's `data` field, and construct `OpenAI(base_url=...)` using the selected backend from `config show`.

Print a handoff block before calling the backend:

```text
inferctl selected: ollama_small / qwen3:8b
reason: selected fallback because primary 'qwen-coder-32b.gguf' is unavailable
data plane: calling http://127.0.0.1:11434 directly
---
```
acceptance_criteria:
- Running `python examples/agent-discovery/demo.py "what is 2+2?"` prints the handoff block before the backend call and prints the model response when the configured backend supports the requested OpenAI-compatible chat/completion endpoint.
- The script reads `route_decision.selected_backend`, `route_decision.selected_model`, and `route_decision.reason` from `preflight --json`.
- The script resolves `effective_config.backends[<selected_backend>].base_url` from `config show --json`.
- JSON diagnostics from failed inferctl commands are surfaced without Python tracebacks for expected user/config errors.
notes: Closed: agent discovery demo completed in commit dfee328. Verified with examples/agent-discovery/test.sh.
close_reason: Added Python agent stub with preflight handoff and direct backend call path — commit dfee328

- The TypeScript script uses `preflight` route-decision fields and `config show` for control-plane state.

title: Add Deterministic Agent Demo Harness
description: The repository needs a deterministic way to exercise the agent handoff without requiring a contributor to have Ollama, llama.cpp, or a large local model running.
design: Add a shell harness that builds inferctl from the checkout, starts `cmd/infer-testserver` instances for primary-unavailable and fallback-ready scenarios, writes an isolated config, runs the Python stub, and checks the handoff output. The repository-local fixture command is `cmd/infer-testserver`; that name is intentional even though the user-facing CLI is `inferctl`. If the fixture server cannot produce a realistic chat/completion response, keep the harness focused on verifying preflight, handoff output, and direct-call target resolution, then document the live-backend response requirement separately.
acceptance_criteria:
- The harness runs from a clean checkout without requiring external local model daemons.
- The harness verifies that fallback selection is printed when the primary backend is unreachable.
- The harness verifies that no prompt text is emitted by inferctl JSON output; only prompt metadata is used by the readiness check.
- The harness exits non-zero if the script selects a backend/model that differs from inferctl's route decision.
- The harness does not claim to verify model quality or response text unless the fixture backend implements the exact endpoint the demo calls.
notes: Closed: agent discovery demo completed in commit dfee328. Verified with examples/agent-discovery/test.sh.
close_reason: Added deterministic fixture harness for fallback handoff and prompt privacy checks — commit dfee328

title: Build Preflight Gate Demo
description:
Agents, eval harnesses, and CI jobs need a cheap deterministic check before they
spend time discovering that the local model stack is unavailable or has fallen
back to a weaker route. The demo should show `inferctl preflight` as the
automation boundary: shell scripts and agents read the exit code; humans read a
concise explanation.
design:
Create `examples/preflight-gate/` with a minimal shell wrapper, a sample task,
deterministic fixture coverage, and README usage. Make the exit code contract
visible without burying users in JSON.

The canonical shape is:

```sh
set -e
inferctl preflight code --prompt-file task.txt --allow-fallback
python agent.py
```

Target exit codes:

```text
0  automation may attempt the task
1  invocation error or malformed caller input
3  config or environment error
4  transient control-plane failure
5  clean negative readiness decision: policy block or no runnable route
```

Preflight should compose config validity, route selection, backend reachability,
selected model readiness, fallback policy, prompt metadata, warnings, and next
diagnostic action. It must not require callers to reimplement inferctl policy.

- `examples/preflight-gate/run-agent.sh` runs `inferctl preflight` before the
  agent command and relies on the preflight exit code.

  according to explicit flags.
- The README leads with the two-line shell shape before explaining JSON.
- The example does not call the model, mutate config, auto-start backends, warm
  models, load models, pull models, or post pull request comments.
- The deterministic harness covers runnable primary, runnable fallback,
  fallback blocked by policy, no route, invalid config, and transient backend
  failure.
- The demo documentation includes the default policy matrix for fallback and
  readiness flags.

title: Preserve Preflight Automation Contract
description:
The exit code is the most important interface. The product and demo need a
stable mapping between control-plane state and automation behavior.
design:
Change and document the exit-code mapping:

- `0`: selected route satisfies the requested policy and automation may attempt
  the task.
- `1`: invocation error or malformed caller input, including missing task,
  unknown task, invalid flag values, and mutually exclusive prompt sources.
- `3`: invalid config, missing required config sources, or environment state
  that prevents a reliable control-plane decision, including unreadable prompt
  files.
- `4`: retryable backend probe or control-plane failure.
- `5`: clean negative readiness decision where inferctl understood the request
  and the local control plane answered "do not run": fallback blocked by policy,
  selected model not ready under `--require-ready`, or a non-transient no-route
  result after route evaluation.

`--allow-fallback` and `--require-ready` are policy inputs. They must not change
the route decision itself and must not cause model loading, warmup, or
inference.
acceptance_criteria:
- `E_PREFLIGHT_POLICY_BLOCKED` exits `5`, not `1`.
- Deterministic no-route readiness failures that are not transient probe
  failures exit `5`, not `1`.
- Contract tests cover every exit code and policy flag combination used by the
  demo.
- Generated verb docs, capabilities, error catalog, and README use the same
  exit-code taxonomy.
- The capabilities or contract version changes with the exit-code taxonomy so
  automation can detect the new behavior.
- Release notes document the pre-1.0 breaking change from policy-block exit `1`
  to clean-negative exit `5`.
- Expected policy failures emit readable human output and structured JSON
  failure data where the verb contract says failure data is emitted.
- The demo wrapper does not parse human output to decide whether to continue.

title: Stabilize Human, JSON, and Markdown Output
description:
Preflight serves humans, agents, and CI summaries. Those surfaces should share
one decision object so they cannot drift.
design:
Ensure the `preflight` report contains task, prompt metadata without prompt
content, selected backend and model, route readiness, fallback status, applied
policy flags, unavailable primary candidate details when safe, warning/error
codes, and recommended action.

Define the readiness ladder once and use the same terms in human output, JSON,
and docs:

```text
configured  a route exists in config for the task
reachable   the selected backend can be contacted
ready       the selected model is already loaded/resident on that backend
```

`--require-ready` applies only to the final rung. Without it, a configured and
reachable route whose model is not resident can still be runnable, with an
explicit not-ready warning.
acceptance_criteria:
- Human, JSON, and Markdown outputs are generated from the same preflight report
  data.
- JSON fixtures cover route decision, runnability, policy flags, warnings, and
  recommended action.
- Human output names fallback state explicitly; it never hides degraded state
  behind a plain success message.
- Human output and JSON use the configured/reachable/ready terms consistently.
- Markdown output omits prompt content and unsafe endpoint details.

Support JSON and Markdown output from the same decision data.
acceptance_criteria:
- `inferctl preflight <task>` is registered in the CLI, capabilities contract, schema export, generated verb docs, and contract goldens.
- The command accepts no prompt, `--prompt-file`, `--prompt`, `--from-stdin`, and the stdin marker where consistent with route; multiple prompt sources are rejected as invocation errors.
- `preflight --json` emits a standard inferctl envelope whose data includes requested task, prompt metadata, route decision, runnability status, applied policy flags, warnings, and recommended action.
- Prompt content is not emitted, persisted, or included in Markdown output.
- Exit codes are 0 when automation may attempt the task, 1 for invocation or policy blocks, 3 for config or environment errors, and 4 for retryable transient control-plane failure.
- `--allow-fallback` and `--require-ready` affect only runnability policy; they do not change route selection and do not warm, load, or query a model.
- `--format markdown` produces a PR/CI-friendly summary from the same control-plane decision data as JSON output.
- Tests cover primary ready, fallback accepted, fallback blocked, selected model not ready with `--require-ready`, unknown task, unreadable prompt file, invalid config, and no route available.

title: Implement file-to-file diff
description: Drift debugging needs a domain-specific explanation of what changed between two known control-plane states. Generic JSON diff is too noisy and cannot rank route changes, fallback status, backend reachability, readiness, warning/error codes, or recommended actions in product terms.
design: Add deterministic file-to-file comparison for snapshot artifacts with `inferctl diff --before <snapshot.json> --after <snapshot.json>`, plus JSON and human output modes. Validate both inputs as comparable snapshot artifacts before classification. Rank domain-specific control-plane changes ahead of generic JSON churn and keep timestamp-only changes ignored or low priority. Defer relative-date lookup until snapshot storage and retention policy exist.
acceptance_criteria:
- `inferctl diff` is registered in the CLI, capabilities contract, schema export, generated verb docs, and contract goldens.
- The MVP supports deterministic file-to-file comparison with `--before` and `--after`.
- `--since yesterday` and other relative-date lookup forms are not part of the MVP unless snapshot storage and retention policy are implemented.
- The command emits a clear invocation or environment error for missing, unreadable, malformed, or incompatible inputs.
- The classifier ranks selected route changes, fallback status changes, backend reachability changes, selected model readiness changes, warning/error code changes, recommended action changes, and loaded model count changes ahead of generic JSON churn.
- Human and JSON output use the same classified change list and severity ordering.
- JSON output preserves machine-readable change type, subject, before value, after value, severity or rank, and explanation.
- Human output concisely explains the highest-impact changes.
- The command does not compare prompt outputs, evaluate model quality, infer answer correctness, or claim that an agent got better or worse.
- Tests cover route change, fallback introduced or removed, backend reachability change, readiness change, warning/error code changes, timestamp-only changes being ignored or deprioritized, malformed input, and incompatible snapshot schema versions.
notes:

COMPLETED:

- Supports raw snapshot JSON and envelope-wrapped snapshots.

- Uses the shared domain classifier for selected route, fallback, backend reachability, readiness, warning/error code, recommended action, and loaded-model-count changes.
- Human and JSON output share the same ranked change list.

title: Implement snapshot command
description: Drift debugging needs a durable artifact that captures inferctl's relevant control-plane view for a task at a point in time. Ad hoc doctor and route JSON files are useful while prototyping, but they do not define a comparable bundle format or privacy policy that future diff logic can rely on.
design: Add `inferctl snapshot --task <task>` with prompt-source support aligned to the shared prompt metadata contract. Persist a purpose-built normalized bundle rather than nested raw command envelopes. Support stdout output by default and `--output <snapshot.json>` for writing a requested path. Ship this with or after deterministic file-to-file diff so the snapshot schema is proven by comparison behavior before it becomes durable.
acceptance_criteria:
- `inferctl snapshot` is registered in the CLI, capabilities contract, schema export, generated verb docs, and contract goldens.
- Snapshot data includes an explicit snapshot schema version.
- Snapshot data includes task, prompt metadata, route decision, route candidates, backend reachability, loaded and installed model summaries, warnings/errors, inferctl version, contract version, and capture timestamp.
- The persisted artifact avoids nested raw command envelopes while reusing existing command data shapes where appropriate.
- Prompt content is redacted by default; the MVP stores only metadata such as source kind, character count, estimated token count, content hash, and filename or basename when relevant.
- `--output <snapshot.json>` writes the artifact to the requested path; without `--output`, the command writes the artifact to stdout.
- Snapshot creation is read-only and does not run inference, warm models, load models, mutate config, or write recurring state.
- Tests cover JSON shape, prompt redaction, output-file writing, unreadable prompt file, invalid config, deterministic mode, and compatibility with the file-to-file diff parser.
notes:

COMPLETED:
- Implemented inferctl snapshot --task with prompt-file, inline prompt, stdin, --output, and --json support.
- Raw snapshot artifacts include snapshot schema version, task, prompt metadata, route decision/candidates, backend reachability, installed and loaded model summaries, warnings/errors, inferctl version, contract version, and capture timestamp.
- Prompt text and local prompt paths are redacted by default; prompt hash and basename are stored when a prompt source is provided.
- --output writes a raw comparable artifact; --json emits the standard envelope while still writing raw output artifacts when requested.
- Added schema, capabilities, generated verb docs, contract golden, deterministic timestamp support, and diff compatibility tests.

title: Add snapshot retention and since lookup
description: Relative-date diff lookup and automatic snapshot history are useful, but they require explicit storage, retention, and privacy policy. Implementing them before the deterministic file-to-file flow would prematurely freeze recurring state under inferctl-managed storage.
design: Design and implement snapshot storage only after the file-to-file diff surface and explicit snapshot artifact are stable. Define where recurring snapshots are stored, what retention policy applies, how prompt metadata privacy is preserved, and how `inferctl diff --since <relative-date>` resolves the correct baseline snapshot.
acceptance_criteria:
- Automatic or recurring snapshot storage has an explicit storage location, retention policy, and privacy policy.
- `inferctl diff --since <relative-date>` resolves comparable stored snapshots using the same classifier as file-to-file diff.
- Stored snapshots continue to redact prompt content by default.
- The implementation handles missing history, multiple candidate snapshots, malformed stored snapshots, and incompatible snapshot schema versions with clear errors.
- Tests cover retention behavior, relative-date lookup, missing baseline, multiple baseline candidates, malformed stored snapshot, and incompatible schema version.

title: Polish Status Dashboard Demo
description:
The status dashboard is the visual demonstration of inferctl as a read-only control plane for local inference. It should show configured backends, exposed and loaded models, route readiness, warnings, recommended actions, and change events without running inference or mutating backend state.

The current product shape is:

```sh
inferctl status --json
inferctl status --json --watch --events --interval 2s
inferctl dashboard --interval 2s
```

`status --json --watch --events` is the machine feed. `dashboard` is the human Bubble Tea interface over that public feed. Automation should consume `status`, not scrape the dashboard. `dashboard --json` should continue to refuse with a structured error.
design: Treat the demo as polish and packaging around the existing status/dashboard boundary. Build a deterministic visual scenario where one backend is reachable, one primary backend becomes unavailable or starts unavailable, and a route falls back to a ready model. The `status` feed should narrate the meaningful changes as event batches, and the dashboard should render those public events rather than deriving private dashboard-only changes.
acceptance_criteria:
- `inferctl status --json` emits an aggregate frame with backend reachability, model inventory, route decisions, warnings, and recommended action.
- `inferctl status --json --watch --events --interval 2s` emits newline-delimited JSON envelopes containing status frames and event batches.
- `inferctl dashboard --interval 2s` renders the same status feed for humans.
- `inferctl dashboard --json` refuses and points machine consumers to `status --json --watch`.
- The dashboard demo remains read-only: no load, pull, start, restart, warmup, chat, completion, embedding, or benchmark action appears in the interface.
notes: Closed: status dashboard demo completed via children inferctl-f9k.1 through inferctl-f9k.5. Implementation commit 1dc7727 polishes dashboard rendering, adds focused tests, deterministic status event scenario, and transcript documentation.
close_reason: Status dashboard demo complete: public-feed rendering, deterministic event scenario, transcript asset, and boundary docs delivered — commit 1dc7727

title: Preserve Status Feed Contract
description: The dashboard is persuasive only if it is visibly backed by a public machine contract rather than by private rendering-only aggregation.
design: Keep dashboard data sourced from `inferctl status --json --watch --events`. Document and test the status frame fields used by the dashboard: summary, backends, models, routes, warnings, and recommended action. Document and test event batch fields: event schema version, captured timestamps, kind, subject, severity, summary, before, and after. Event summaries should be produced by the shared control-plane change classification used by status events and `inferctl diff`, not by ad-hoc dashboard-only comparison logic.
acceptance_criteria:
- Dashboard code consumes the public status watch feed rather than calling private aggregation paths directly.
- Contract goldens cover representative `status` frames and event batches.
- Documentation tells automation users to consume `status --json --watch`, not `dashboard`.
- Event summaries come from shared control-plane change classification rather than dashboard-specific diffing.
- The dashboard can be reasoned about as a renderer over the public feed.
notes: Closed: status dashboard demo polish completed in commit 1dc7727. Verified with go test ./cmd/inferctl -run 'TestDashboard|TestStatusEvents' and examples/status-dashboard/test.sh.
close_reason: Kept dashboard rendering over public status watch/event feed and tested shared event semantics — commit 1dc7727

title: Improve Human Dashboard Rendering
description: The dashboard needs to be legible in a terminal recording and useful for a human scanning local inference state.
design:
Polish the Bubble Tea view around dense operator information:

- Summary line: reachable backends, loaded/exposed models, ready routes, warning count.
- Backends table: status, name, kind, URL.
- Routes table: task, selected backend/model, ready status, fallback status.
- Events section: recent status event summaries ordered by recency.

Avoid mutation affordances. The interface should not display commands as buttons or imply that actions can be taken inside the dashboard.
acceptance_criteria:
- The dashboard fits cleanly in a typical terminal recording viewport.
- Route fallback state and backend unreachable state are visible without reading JSON.
- Recent events remain bounded so the UI does not grow indefinitely.
- The view contains no controls that mutate backend or model state.
notes: Closed: status dashboard demo polish completed in commit 1dc7727. Verified with go test ./cmd/inferctl -run 'TestDashboard|TestStatusEvents' and examples/status-dashboard/test.sh.
close_reason: Improved dashboard view with compact tables, fallback visibility, newest-first events, and read-only footer — commit 1dc7727

title: Add Deterministic Dashboard Scenario
description: Marketing and regression checks need a repeatable way to produce dashboard frames and event batches without relying on a contributor's personal local LLM setup.
design:
Use repository fixture servers to create a two-backend scenario:

- Primary llama.cpp-style backend is unreachable or transitions to unreachable.
- Fallback Ollama-style backend is reachable and has a loaded model.
- A configured route selects the fallback and emits warnings.

Capture both `status --json` and `status --json --watch --events` output for validation and demo recording. Verify event wording through the shared control-plane change classification path so status events and `inferctl diff` do not drift apart.
acceptance_criteria:
- The scenario runs from a source checkout without real local model daemons.
- The scenario produces at least one route fallback warning.
- The scenario produces at least one status event batch when state changes are simulated.
- The scenario verifies that event summaries describe domain changes, not generic JSON diffs.
- The scenario verifies that event summaries are visible in the `status --json --watch --events` stream before the dashboard renders them.
notes: Closed: status dashboard demo polish completed in commit 1dc7727. Verified with go test ./cmd/inferctl -run 'TestDashboard|TestStatusEvents' and examples/status-dashboard/test.sh.
close_reason: Added deterministic status-dashboard scenario with status frames and public event batches — commit 1dc7727

title: Produce Dashboard Demo Asset
description: The status dashboard is the most visual demo, so it needs a reproducible terminal recording or GIF that can be used in README and launch materials.
design:
Record a short terminal sequence that shows:

1. `inferctl status --json | jq '.data.summary'`
2. `inferctl status --json --watch --events --interval 2s`
3. `inferctl dashboard --interval 2s`

Keep the recording focused on read-only observation and route drift. Avoid showing local absolute paths, private hostnames, credentials, or personal machine details.
acceptance_criteria:
- The recording is reproducible from documented commands.
- The recording shows a backend/route change or fallback event.
- The recording does not expose private paths, endpoints, tokens, or machine-specific details beyond loopback fixture URLs.
- README or docs link to the generated asset.
notes: Closed: status dashboard demo polish completed in commit 1dc7727. Verified with go test ./cmd/inferctl -run 'TestDashboard|TestStatusEvents' and examples/status-dashboard/test.sh.
close_reason: Added reproducible dashboard transcript asset linked from scenario docs — commit 1dc7727

title: Document Dashboard Boundary
description: The dashboard can blur inferctl's positioning if it looks like a control panel. The docs need to make the boundary explicit.
design: Document that `status` and `dashboard` are read-only control-plane surfaces. They inspect config, backend reachability, model inventory, route decisions, warnings, and recommendations. They do not run inference, load models, warm models, pull models, start daemons, or repair state.
acceptance_criteria:
- Docs state that automation should use `status --json --watch`.
- Docs state that `dashboard` is for humans and refuses `--json`.
- Docs state that status events are derived from consecutive status frames and that dashboard renders those public event batches.
- Docs avoid lifecycle-management language such as "fix", "start", "pull", or "load" as dashboard actions.
notes: Closed: status dashboard demo polish completed in commit 1dc7727. Verified with go test ./cmd/inferctl -run 'TestDashboard|TestStatusEvents' and examples/status-dashboard/test.sh.
close_reason: Documented dashboard boundary and machine-consumer status feed guidance in scenario docs — commit 1dc7727

title: Build Routing Explainer Demo
description:
Users need to understand why inferctl selected one local model route instead of
another. The demo should make that invisible control-plane decision visible in a
single terminal screen:

```sh
inferctl route code --prompt-file task.txt --explain
```

The product surface is the CLI explanation, not a web UI. The example should be
terminal-native, README-friendly, and runnable without local web
infrastructure.
design:
Create `examples/routing-explainer/` with a sample task, README, optional
terminal recording instructions, and deterministic output coverage. The demo
should show route candidates, selected backend/model, fallback status, prompt
metadata, unavailable candidate reasons, warning codes, and the next diagnostic
command.

Target human output after renderer polish:

```text
selected: qwen3:8b on ollama_small (fallback)
reason:   primary backend unreachable

candidates:
  role      backend          model                 status
  primary   llamacpp_large   qwen-coder-32b.gguf   unavailable
  fallback  ollama_small     qwen3:8b              selected

prompt:
  45 estimated tokens / 8192 max context tokens

next: inferctl backends --filter llamacpp_large --json
```
acceptance_criteria:
- `examples/routing-explainer/README.md` leads with
  `inferctl route code --prompt-file sample-task.txt --explain`.
- The example does not include React, a local server, browser UI, chat UI, or
  model streaming.
- Human output fits in a typical terminal recording and shows the selected
  route, fallback status, candidate table, prompt metadata, and next diagnostic.
- README packaging does not ship until the renderer output matches the target
  shape and current goldens.
- JSON output exposes the same decision for advanced users and tests.
- Deterministic coverage proves route explanation output without requiring real
  local model daemons.

title: Polish Terminal Explanation Format
description:
The terminal explanation is the whole product experience for this demo. It
needs to be dense, stable, and readable without any external UI.
design:
Use a fixed section order:

1. compact selected-route summary
2. reason for selection
3. candidate table
4. prompt metadata and context budget, when a prompt source is provided
5. warnings
6. next diagnostic command, when the route report or command diagnostics expose
   one

The candidate table should make primary/fallback roles and unavailable reasons
visually obvious. Avoid color as the only signal so recordings and plain logs
remain useful.

Command recommendations must not surface commands that the current binary cannot
run. If a command diagnostic includes `AvailableInVersion` greater than the
current tool version, the human `next:` line must skip it and choose the first
currently available recommendation.
acceptance_criteria:
- Output has a stable section order across primary-ready, fallback-selected,
  and no-route states.
- The current flat candidate list is replaced by the structured candidate table
  before README samples are captured.
- Candidate rows include role, backend, model, and status/reason.
- Fallback selection is explicitly labeled in the summary and candidate table.
- Prompt content is never printed; only metadata is shown.
- The `next:` line skips recommendations whose `AvailableInVersion` is newer
  than the current binary.
- Long model/backend names do not make the output unreadable in an 80- to
  100-column terminal.
notes:
2026-07-01 CLOSED:
COMPLETED:
- Replaced flat route human output with stable selected/reason/candidates/prompt/warnings/next sections.
- Added structured candidate table with role, backend, model, and status/reason.
- Added no-route terminal explanation with candidate table and current diagnostic command.
- Human next command skips future-version commands by selecting the first currently available command.
- Added command-level tests for fallback human output, no-route human output, prompt metadata redaction, and future-command filtering.
VERIFICATION:
- go test ./cmd/inferctl -run "TestRoute|TestPreflight"
- scripts/check-contract-goldens.sh
BLOCKERS:

title: Preserve JSON and Explain Parity
description:
The human explanation must not become a second implementation of routing
policy. It should render the same decision object as `route --json`.
design:
Keep route explanation data normalized around requested task, prompt metadata,
selected backend and model, fallback status, readiness status, candidates
considered, candidate rejection/unavailable reasons, warnings/errors, and
command recommendations.

Human output may omit low-level fields, but every visible claim should map back
to the JSON route explanation.
acceptance_criteria:
- Golden tests compare human output claims against `route --json` fixture data.
- The selected backend/model in human output always matches JSON output.
- Candidate count and statuses in human output match JSON output.
- Recommended action in README samples comes from real route/preflight command
  diagnostics for the fixture scenario, not hard-coded demo text.

title: Add Routing Explainer Example
description:
The example should be a thin package around the product command, suitable for a
README and terminal recording.
design:
Create:

```text
examples/routing-explainer/
  README.md
  sample-task.txt
```

If a script is included, it should only wrap:

```sh
inferctl route code --prompt-file sample-task.txt --explain
```

The README should include one primary-ready output sample and one
fallback-selected output sample. The fallback sample is the stronger demo
because it shows inferctl explaining a surprising route. Any `next:` line in the
sample must be captured from the current binary or generated fixture output.
acceptance_criteria:
- Example can be run with one command from the example directory.
- README includes expected output captured after the renderer polish lands and
  matches current goldens.

title: Build Agent Drift Debug Demo
description:
When a local agent suddenly produces worse answers, users often blame the
prompt, application code, or the model. Sometimes the real cause is simpler:
inferctl selected a different local route because backend reachability, loaded
models, fallback policy, or config changed. The demo should show inferctl
capturing comparable control-plane snapshots and explaining the meaningful
differences.
design:
Create `examples/agent-drift-debug/` with snapshot fixtures generated from the
real `inferctl snapshot` command and then normalized for deterministic docs. Do
not hand-author snapshot JSON.

Canonical deterministic demo:

```sh
inferctl diff \
  --before fixtures/last-good.snapshot.json \
  --after fixtures/today.snapshot.json
```

Optional live capture flow:

```sh
inferctl snapshot --task code --prompt-file task.txt --output last-good.snapshot.json
inferctl snapshot --task code --prompt-file task.txt --output today.snapshot.json
inferctl diff --before last-good.snapshot.json --after today.snapshot.json
```

The strongest default scenario has yesterday's task selecting
`qwen-coder-32b.gguf` on `llamacpp_large`, today's task falling back to
`qwen3:8b` on `ollama_small` because the primary backend is unreachable, and
loaded model counts changing. Add a second before/after fixture pair with config
change or model readiness drift so the demo is not only a reachability story.
acceptance_criteria:
- `examples/agent-drift-debug/README.md` leads with the deterministic fixture
  diff command.
- Fixtures demonstrate a selected-route change caused by backend reachability
  drift.
- Fixtures include a second scenario where drift is caused by config change or
  model readiness change rather than backend reachability.
- Human diff output ranks route/fallback/reachability changes ahead of generic
  timestamp or JSON churn.
- README packaging does not show grouped narrative output as current behavior
  until the grouped renderer matches goldens.
- JSON diff output exposes machine-readable change type, subject, before value,
  after value, severity/rank, and explanation.
- The demo never runs inference, compares model outputs, judges answer quality,
  installs models, starts daemons, warms models, or mutates config.

title: Stabilize Diff Classification
description: The diff output must explain domain changes, not dump generic JSON deltas.
design:
Rank and classify changes in this order:

1. selected route changed
2. fallback introduced or removed
3. selected backend reachability changed
4. selected model readiness changed
5. warning/error codes appeared or disappeared
6. recommended action changed
7. loaded/installed model inventory changed
8. low-signal metadata changed

The classifier should be designed as a reusable module so future live status
events and dashboard summaries can share the same transition ranking. Completion
of this demo does not depend on dashboard or live-status event integration.
acceptance_criteria:
- Route changes are explained with before/after backend and model.
- Fallback changes name whether fallback was introduced or removed.
- Reachability changes identify the backend and reason code when available.
- Timestamp-only diffs are ignored or ranked below domain changes.
- Human and JSON outputs use the same ordered classified change list.
- Completion of this demo does not depend on dashboard or live-status event
  integration.

title: Document Drift Workflow
description:
The README should teach the troubleshooting move without overstating what
inferctl can prove.
design:
Lead with the deterministic fixture diff, then show live snapshot capture as an
optional workflow. Include a plain-language explanation:

```text
Your agent did not necessarily get worse. Its local inference route changed.
```

Use "necessarily" or equivalent wording because inferctl can explain
control-plane drift but cannot prove model output quality. Cross-link
`route --explain` for current route reasoning, `preflight` for yes/no
automation readiness, and live status/dashboard surfaces for state observation.
acceptance_criteria:
- README does not claim inferctl measures answer quality or model intelligence.
- README explains snapshots as redacted control-plane artifacts.

title: Build Editor Config Generator Demo
description:
Developers running local LLMs often paste the same backend URL and model name into multiple editor and agent configuration files. Those values drift when a backend changes, a fallback route is selected, or a machine has a different local setup. The demo should show inferctl as the source of truth for local routing and connection metadata, while editors remain the clients that call the backend directly.

The first version should target Aider and Cline. Two targets are enough to demonstrate "one source, many readers" without turning the demo into an editor-integration matrix. Continue.dev should not be a launch target because it has been acquired by Cursor and its original repository has moved to a final-release/read-only posture; it can remain a legacy recipe outside the launch path.
design:
Create `examples/editor-configs/` with a small generator and target-specific templates or adapters. The generator should consume:

```sh
inferctl route code --json
inferctl config show --json
```

`route --json` supplies the selected backend, selected model, fallback status, and reason. `config show --json` supplies backend connection metadata such as `base_url`. `capabilities --json` can be an optional compatibility check for required fields, but it must not be used as the source of live routing state.

Canonical JSON paths for this demo:

- `route --json`: `data.decision.selected_backend`, `data.decision.selected_model`, `data.decision.is_fallback`, `data.decision.ready`, and `data.decision.reason`.
- `config show --json`: `data.effective_config.backends[<selected_backend>].base_url`.

The generator should be one-shot: run it when the developer wants to refresh editor config. It should not watch files, sync continuously, or start a daemon.
acceptance_criteria:
- The demo can generate an Aider config and a Cline OpenAI-compatible settings recipe from the same inferctl route/config state.
- Generated artifacts include the selected model and selected backend URL required by each editor.
- If inferctl selected a fallback route, the generator prints a visible warning before or alongside the generated config.
- The generator does not mutate unrelated editor settings; it writes or prints only the inferctl-managed local model stanza.
- The demo documentation states that inferctl emits configuration and the editor sends inference traffic directly to its configured backend.
notes: Closed: editor-config demo completed via children inferctl-oe3.1 through inferctl-oe3.5. Implementation commit 6806f2d adds the generator, Aider output, Cline recipe, degraded-route diagnostics, and deterministic fixture harness.
close_reason: Editor config generator demo complete: generator CLI, Aider/Cline outputs, degraded-route diagnostics, and fixture harness delivered — commit 6806f2d

title: Define Generator CLI
description: The demo needs a small, repeatable command surface so users can understand and rerun config generation without reading implementation details.
design:
Implement a wrapper such as:

```sh
examples/editor-configs/generate.py --target aider
examples/editor-configs/generate.py --target cline
examples/editor-configs/generate.py --target aider --output .aider.conf.yml
```

The generator should call inferctl itself rather than requiring users to prepare temporary JSON files. Use explicit target names and an optional output path. If no output path is given, print the generated config or settings recipe to stdout and diagnostics to stderr.
acceptance_criteria:
- `--target aider` and `--target cline` are supported.
- Unknown targets fail with a non-zero exit and list valid targets.
- stdout remains a parseable generated config or structured settings recipe when diagnostics are emitted.
- Existing output files require an explicit overwrite behavior or a documented safe default.

title: Generate Cline Settings Recipe
description: Cline is the second launch target because it is an active local-first coding-agent tool and its OpenAI-compatible provider settings map directly to inferctl's selected backend URL and model.
design:
Add a Cline adapter that emits a clipboard-ready settings recipe for Cline's OpenAI-compatible provider. The recipe should include:

- API Provider: `OpenAI Compatible`.
- Base URL: selected backend `base_url`.
- Model ID: selected model.
- API key handling: placeholder-only unless the backend requires no key or a documented dummy key.

If Cline exposes a stable importable config file or CLI configuration path, the adapter can emit that format. If not, the first version should emit a structured setup block that is easy to paste into docs or a terminal recording without pretending to mutate Cline settings automatically.

- The emitted recipe contains the selected model from `route --json`.
- The emitted recipe contains the selected backend's `base_url` from `config show --json`.
- The emitted recipe does not print secrets or infer credential values from inferctl config.
- The generated output is deterministic for the same inferctl route/config input.

title: Build Team Requirements Verify Demo
description:
Teams need a repo-level way to declare the local inference state a project expects and to fail fast when a teammate or CI runner has drifted from that expectation. The demo should ship as an external example that compares a committed requirements file against inferctl's read-only live-state outputs.

The first version should use a hand-written `inferctl.requirements.toml`, not a generated lockfile. Requirements express author intent. A future lockfile would be a derived snapshot, but the demo does not need `inferctl lock` or `inferctl verify` to be useful.

Current inferctl primitives are sufficient:

```sh
inferctl config validate --json
inferctl preflight code --prompt-file prompt.txt --allow-fallback --json
inferctl status --json
```

The verifier must report drift only. It must not pull models, start daemons, load models, rewrite inferctl config, or attempt repair.
design:
Create `examples/team-requirements/` containing:

- `inferctl.requirements.toml`, a small committed intent file.
- `verify-local-llm.py`, a read-only verifier.
- A prompt fixture used only for prompt metadata in `preflight`.
- A README with local and CI usage.

The verifier should parse requirements, run inferctl JSON commands, compare expected task readiness/backend/model properties to actual state, and exit non-zero with a readable report when requirements are not satisfied.

This plan assumes an inferctl build that includes `preflight`. If the verifier runs against an older binary without `preflight`, it should fail with a clear version/compatibility message rather than silently downgrading task-readiness checks. Backend presence, reachability, and model inventory still come from `status --json`; task runnability and fallback policy come from `preflight --json`.
acceptance_criteria:
- Running `python examples/team-requirements/verify-local-llm.py` exits zero when local state satisfies the requirements file.
- Missing backend reachability, missing model availability, non-runnable preflight state, and unexpected fallback policy each produce non-zero exits with readable messages.
- The verifier uses inferctl JSON envelopes and checks `ok`, `errors`, `warnings`, and `data` intentionally.
- The verifier performs no mutation and emits no commands that install, start, load, or repair backend state.
- Documentation explains the requirements-vs-lockfile distinction and names future `lock`/`verify` verbs only as possible graduation paths, not dependencies.
notes: Closed: team requirements verify demo completed via children inferctl-t4j.1 through inferctl-t4j.5. Implementation commit 06eb21c adds requirements TOML, verifier, CI/docs, deterministic harness, and graduation-path documentation.
close_reason: Team requirements verify demo complete: requirements file, read-only verifier, CI docs, harness, and graduation-path docs delivered — commit 06eb21c

title: Define Requirements TOML
description: The demo needs a human-editable requirements format that captures the small set of checks teams actually need at first: task readiness, allowed fallback behavior, selected model expectations, and backend reachability.
design:
Start narrow with a TOML shape like:

```toml
[tasks.code]
prompt_file = "prompt.txt"
selected_model_any_of = ["qwen-coder-32b.gguf", "qwen3:8b"]
allow_fallback = true
require_ready = false

[backends.ollama]
required = true
require_reachable = true
```

Avoid endpoint URL assertions in the first version unless needed for the demo fixture; endpoint values can be sensitive or machine-specific.
acceptance_criteria:
- The requirements file is valid TOML and readable without generated comments.
- Task requirements can specify prompt fixture, allowed selected models, fallback policy, and readiness policy.
- Backend requirements can specify required presence and reachability.
- The format avoids local absolute paths and credentials.
notes: Closed: team requirements verifier completed in commit 06eb21c. Verified with examples/team-requirements/test.sh.
close_reason: Added human-editable inferctl.requirements.toml and prompt fixture — commit 06eb21c

title: Implement Read-Only Verifier
description: The verifier is the core artifact: it turns inferctl's live-state contracts into a team-friendly pass/fail report.
design:
Implement `examples/team-requirements/verify-local-llm.py`. The script should:

- Load `inferctl.requirements.toml`.
- Run `inferctl config validate --json`.
- Run `inferctl preflight <task> --prompt-file <file> --json`, adding `--allow-fallback` and `--require-ready` according to the task requirement.
- Run `inferctl status --json` for backend/model aggregate checks.
- Compare actual state to requirements.
- Print a concise pass/fail report.
acceptance_criteria:
- The script exits non-zero if config validation returns errors.
- The script exits non-zero if a required backend is absent or unreachable.
- The script exits non-zero if the selected model is not in the task's allowed model set.
- The script exits non-zero if fallback selection violates the task fallback policy.
- The script exits zero and prints a pass summary when all checks pass.
notes: Closed: team requirements verifier completed in commit 06eb21c. Verified with examples/team-requirements/test.sh.
close_reason: Added read-only verifier using config validate, status, and preflight JSON contracts — commit 06eb21c

description: The team story lands when the same verifier can run locally and in CI as a preflight check before agent automation.
design: Document a minimal GitHub Actions snippet that installs inferctl with `go install`, prepares any expected local test backend fixture if applicable, and runs the verifier. Keep the snippet clearly framed as an example, not a hosted service or fleet dashboard.
acceptance_criteria:
- The README includes a copyable CI snippet.
- The snippet does not include secrets, private endpoints, or model-pull commands.
- The README explains that CI can also use deterministic fixture backends when real local model daemons are unavailable.

close_reason: Documented local and CI usage for requirements verification — commit 06eb21c

title: Add Deterministic Verify Harness
description: The example needs a deterministic pass/fail harness so future changes to inferctl contracts or verifier behavior do not silently break the demo.
design: Use `cmd/infer-testserver` or fixture JSON envelopes to exercise success and failure cases. The repository-local fixture command is `cmd/infer-testserver`; that name is intentional even though the user-facing CLI is `inferctl`. Cover a reachable fallback backend, an unreachable primary backend, and a requirements mismatch.
acceptance_criteria:
- The harness runs without requiring real Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio, MLX, or remote endpoints.
- At least one passing and one failing requirements scenario are covered.
- Failure output includes specific drift reasons rather than only "verification failed."
- The harness remains read-only with respect to inferctl config and backend state.

close_reason: Added deterministic verifier harness with passing and failing drift scenarios — commit 06eb21c

title: Document Graduation Path
description: The demo's filename and analogy may invite readers to expect lockfile behavior. The docs need to prevent that confusion while preserving the long-term product direction.
design:
Add a short section that distinguishes requirements from lockfiles:

- `inferctl.requirements.toml` is author intent and hand-editable.
- A future lockfile would be a generated snapshot of a known-good local inference state.
- The external verifier can graduate into a first-class `inferctl verify` only if the requirements pattern proves useful.
acceptance_criteria:
- The docs do not imply that `inferctl lock` or `inferctl verify` exists today.
- The docs explain why verify reports drift instead of fixing it.
- The docs keep inferctl positioned as a read-only control-plane tool, not a package manager or provisioner.

close_reason: Documented requirements-vs-lockfile graduation path without implying lock/verify verbs exist — commit 06eb21c

title: Build PR / CI Markdown Summary Demo
description:
Teams running local LLM automation on self-hosted runners need a lightweight way
to record which local inference route was available for a job. The demo should
show inferctl producing a safe Markdown summary that can be published by an
existing CI system, without giving inferctl ownership of pull request comments
or any provider API.

This is a supporting CI packaging example. Its value is operational
convenience: it places the existing preflight Markdown in the CI surface where
teams already look. The example must demonstrate a formatter, not a bot.
design:
Create `examples/ci-markdown-summary/` with CI snippets and deterministic
fixtures. Lead with a GitHub Actions step summary because it is copyable and
does not require pull request write permission, while keeping the Markdown
provider-neutral enough for Buildkite, GitLab CI, and any runner that can
publish build annotations.

Use the existing product output directly:

```sh
inferctl preflight code_review \
  --prompt-file .github/pr-review-prompt.txt \
  --format markdown >> "$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY"
```

The Markdown contract is owned by `preflight` and its product tests. This
example consumes the output exactly as a user would.

The summary should include task name, runnability status, selected backend and
model, fallback status, the highest-impact unavailable primary candidate when
safe, safe warning/error codes, a recommended follow-up command, and a short
privacy note when prompt content is intentionally omitted.
acceptance_criteria:
- `examples/ci-markdown-summary/README.md` contains a minimal GitHub Actions
  snippet that appends `inferctl preflight ... --format markdown` to
  `$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY`.
- The example does not include a pull request comment bot, provider API client,
  sticky-comment logic, `gh pr comment`, or any required pull request write
  permission.
- The generated Markdown shows selected backend, selected model, fallback
  status, runnability, warning codes, and recommended diagnostic action.
- Documentation explains how users can pipe the same Markdown into their own CI
  annotation or PR-comment tooling if they choose.
- The demo is deterministic from fixture config/test backends or fixture JSON
  golden output and does not require a public pull request to verify.
- The demo does not define or fork the Markdown layout; it verifies that
  existing preflight Markdown can be redirected into CI surfaces.
- CI documentation calls out current-versus-target exit-code behavior if the
  preflight exit-5 remap has not shipped yet.
